question: A void @Async method throws a RuntimeException. What happens if no AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler is configured?
options: The exception propagates to the calling thread and the caller handles it
Spring retries the async method automatically up to 3 times
The exception is silently swallowed — no stack trace, no log entry
The application context shuts down to prevent data corruption

question: What is the difference between @Scheduled(fixedRate = 5000) and @Scheduled(fixedDelay = 5000)?
options: They are identical — both wait 5 seconds between executions
fixedRate starts the next run 5s after the previous run STARTED; fixedDelay starts 5s after the previous run COMPLETED
fixedRate is for cron-style schedules; fixedDelay is for interval-based schedules
fixedDelay runs in a separate thread; fixedRate runs on the main thread

question: You call an @Async method from within the same @Service class. What actually happens?
options: The method runs asynchronously as expected because @Async uses bytecode weaving
Spring throws an AsyncException because self-invocation is not allowed
The method runs synchronously — the Spring proxy is bypassed by the direct method call
Spring creates a new thread but still blocks the caller until it completes

question: Which return type should an @Async method use when the caller needs to know the result of the background operation?
options: Optional<T> — allows the caller to check if a value is present
Future<T> or CompletableFuture<T> — allows the caller to retrieve the result when ready
ResponseEntity<T> — the standard Spring wrapper for async results
